coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of iron porphyrin-based porous organic polymer with excellent oxidase-like activity for colorimetric detection of sulfide
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Linhong
Guo, Ding
Luo, Xiaogang
Meng, Lingkui
Wu, Fengshou
coredata.dc:description: The high concentration of sulfide in environment does great harm to human beings’ health due to its toxicity, corrosivity, and disagreeable odor. Therefore, it's highly urgent to develop a simple, selective, and sensitive method for sulfide ion detection. In this work, we synthesized an iron porphyrin-based porous organic polymer (Fe-PPOP) through a facile Friedel-Crafts reaction. The structure of Fe-PPOP was confirmed through a series of methods, such as FT-IR, SEM, EDS, TEM, XPS and nitrogen physisorption. Fe-PPOP exhibited high oxidase-like activity, which could catalyze the oxidization of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue product (ox-TMB) in the presence of oxygen. With the addition of sulfide ion to the above system, the color of solution gradually changed from blue to colorless. Therefore, the Fe-PPOP/TMB system could be used for sulfide detection through a facile colorimetric method. The interference experiments using other similar ions verified the high selectivity of Fe-PPOP for sulfide detection. The limit of detection (LOD) of sulfide was calculated to be 1.08 μM. This work provided a promising method for sulfide measurement in environmental pollutants with high reliability and accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfide detection
Porphyrin
Porous organic polymer
Oxidase-like activity

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mimetic oxidase activity assays and kinetic measurements
Study on the possible catalytic mechanism of Fe-PPOP
Determination of sulfide in real samples
Kinetic analysis of Fe-PPOP as oxidase mimics
Mechanism study of the catalytic activity of Fe-PPOP
Selectivity of the sulfide colorimetric determination method
Determination of sulfide in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( NSFC ) (grant no. 21601142 ), the Open Project of Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Engineering Process of Ministry of Education (grant no. GCP202116 ), Outstanding Young and Middle-aged Scientific Innovation Team of Colleges and Universities of Hubei Province : “Biomass chemical technologies and materials” (grant no. T201908), the Innovation Project of Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry ( 2022BEEA07 ), and the second batch of the Key Research and Development Project of Hubei Province (grant no. 2020BAB073 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of Fe-TPP and Fe-PPOP; (b) Electron image and elemental mapping images of C, N and Fe in Fe-PPOP; (c) SEM image of Fe-PPOP; (d) TEM image of Fe-PPOP; (e) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms of Fe-PPOP; (f) Pore size distribution of Fe-PPOP by DFT modeling.
(a) XPS survey of Fe-PPOP; (b) High resolution C 1 s XPS spectra of Fe-PPOP; (c) High resolution N 1 s XPS spectra of Fe-PPOP; (d) High resolution Fe 2p XPS spectra of Fe-PPOP.

(a) Oxidation reaction from TMB to ox-TMB catalyzed by Fe-PPOP in the presence of O2; (b) UV–vis spectra of different solution systems catalyzed by Fe-PPOP, Co-PPOP and Zn-PPOP, respectively (The inset shows the color changes of the TMB oxidation); (c) The comparing experiment of oxidase-like like activity between Fe-PPOP and Fe-TPP (The inset shows the color changes of the TMB oxidation).
Steady-state kinetic assays of Fe-PPOP. (a) Michaelis-Menten curve for Fe-PPOP by varying the TMB concentration; (b) Lineweaver-Burk curve of Fe-PPOP.

(a) The mechanism of S2- detection; (b) Effect of S2- on absorption spectra of ox-TMB in the Fe-PPOP system (the insert photograph is the related solution systems after adding 40, 80, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 μM S2-, respectively (from left to right)); (c) Curve for S2- detection in the range of 15–300 μM (the amount of S2- added was 200 μL).
Synthesis of Fe-PPOP through Friedel-Crafts alkylation reaction between dimethoxymethane and Fe-TPP.
Comparison of the Michaelis-Menten constant of Fe-PPOP with other catalysts.
Comparison of the present method with previous methods for S2- detection.
Determination of sulfide ions in lake water, waste water and Industrial sewage by the proposed method.

row.entry: Concentration of sulfide ions ( μ M)

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of iron porphyrin-based porous organic polymer with excellent oxidase-like activity for colorimetric detection of sulfide

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The high concentration of sulfide in environment does great harm to human beings’ health due to its toxicity, corrosivity, and disagreeable odor. Therefore, it's highly urgent to develop a simple, selective, and sensitive method for sulfide ion detection. In this work, we synthesized an iron porphyrin-based porous organic polymer (Fe-PPOP) through a facile Friedel-Crafts reaction. The structure of Fe-PPOP was confirmed through a series of methods, such as FT-IR, SEM, EDS, TEM, XPS and nitrogen physisorption. Fe-PPOP exhibited high oxidase-like activity, which could catalyze the oxidization of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue product (ox-TMB) in the presence of oxygen. With the addition of sulfide ion to the above system, the color of solution gradually changed from blue to colorless. Therefore, the Fe-PPOP/TMB system could be used for sulfide detection through a facile colorimetric method. The interference experiments using other similar ions verified the high selectivity of Fe-PPOP for sulfide detection. The limit of detection (LOD) of sulfide was calculated to be 1.08 μM. This work provided a promising method for sulfide measurement in environmental pollutants with high reliability and accuracy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, sulfides have been largely released into the natural environment as the byproducts in leather, medicine petrochemical and food processing industries [1]. They are main factor as traditional harmful environmental contaminants and mainly in the form of sulfide ions [2]. If these contaminants are discharged directly, the water may be polluted as the sulfide ions could cause several problems such as irritation in mucous membranes, unconsciousness and dizziness [3,4]. To data, a series of analytical techniques have been developed for the measurement of sulfide ions, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], electrochemistry [6,7], fluorimetery [8,9], and chemiluminescence (CL) [10]. Although these methods offer high accuracy, they still had some shortcomings, such as expensiveness, unsuitability for online analysis, and complicated operation, which greatly limited their practical use. Therefore, it’s of great significance to construct a sensitive, efficient and economic method for real-time determination of sulfides in the real industrial wastewater. Recently, the colorimetric methods have received widespread attention as they were demonstrated to have the advantages of simplicity, rapidity, low cost, and no requirement of any complicated pretreatment procedures and complex equipements [11,12]. This inspired us to explore the colorimetric sensors for sulfide detection.

Natural enzymes play an important role in biochemistry because of their excellent substrate specificity and high catalytic performance under mild environment. However, most of natural enzymes have some problems that limited their practical use, such as the requirement of mild temperature and pH value, low stability, difficult preparation, complicated handling and decreasing catalytic activity under stringent conditions [13]. Therefore, great efforts had been dedicated to develop enzyme mimics with excellent catalytic performance similar to that of natural enzymes. In 2007, Yan et al. first reported that Fe3O4 NPs exhibited peroxidase activity [14]. Since then, various cost-effective nanomaterials have been found to have the peroxidase-like properties, such as metal-organic frameworks (MIL-68 [15], and MIL-53 (Fe) [16]), metallic oxide nanoparticles (GeO2 [17], CeO2 [18], and CuO [19]), carbon dots (N-CQDs [20] and N,S-CDs [21]), and porous polymers (PPN-24 (Fe) [22]). They had been widely used for colorimetric determination of H2O2 [23], ascorbic acid (AA) [24], glutathione (GSH) [25] and metal ions [26].

Porous organic polymers (POPs) have drawn increasing attention among all kinds of porous materials because of their superior properties such as facile preparation, tunable pore structures, large surface area, and high chemical- and thermal-stability, which are extensively applied in catalysis, gas storage, adsorption and separation [27]. Porphyrin and its derivatives exhibit high stability because of their large conjugate structure [28]. They are generally used as precursors to construct the porous materials, such as covalent organic framework (COF), metal organic framework (MOF), and porous organic polymer (POP), because of their tunable photophysical and photochemical properties [29–31]. Most importantly, the rational design by introducing the metalloporphyrin complexes into POPs frameworks can endow them with catalytic performances [32,33]. However, the development of POPs with peroxidase or oxidase behavior for colorimetric sensing of sulfides is yet to be reported.

In this context, here we designed an iron porphyrin-based porous organic polymer (Fe-PPOP) as an oxidase mimic for colorimetric detection of sulfide ions. Fe-PPOP was synthesized through Friedel-Crafts alkylation reaction with dimethoxymethane and 5,10,15,20-tetraphenylporphyrin iron complex as starting materials ( Scheme 1). Fe-PPOP displayed excellent oxidase-like activity, which could convert colorless substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into a blue substrate (ox-TMB) in a few minutes. After the addition of sodium sulfide into the Fe-PPOP/TMB system, the blue solution gradually turned into colorless. Based on this phenomenon, a convenient and selective colorimetric method was constructed to detect sulfide ion. Finally, this colorimetric method based on Fe-PPOP was applied to analyze the concentration of S2- in real water samples.

Pyrrole, benzaldehyde, anhydrous ferric chloride, dimethoxymethane, sodium sulfide, and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were obtained from Aladdin (China). Other reagents and chemicals were used as received without any purification. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were carried out on JEOL630-F microscope and JEM-3010 instrument (JEOL), respectively. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of Fe-TPP and Fe-PPOP were recorded by spectrometer with KBr pellets. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed through multifunctional imaging electron spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB). Thermogravimetric analytical (TGA) data were obtained on a Mettler Toledo TGA/SDTA 851°. ASAP 2020 (V 4.01 G) apparatus was used to gain N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, and the surface area of Fe-PPOP was obtained by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) model.

Tetraphenyl-porphyrin (TPP) was synthesized from pyrrole and benzaldehyde according to the literature method [34]. Typically, to a 500 mL two-necked flask, 200 mL propionic acid and benzaldehyde (7.75 g, 73 mmol) were added. After heated to 120 °C, 5 mL (73 mmol) pyrrole in 50 mL propionic acid were then added dropwise to the above solution. The reaction system was cooled to room temperature naturally after refluxing for 2.5 h and stored at refrigerator overnight. The purple powder was collected by filtration and washed with methanol and water sequentially to obtain tetraphenylporphyrin (TPP) (1.9 g, 15 %). 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3), 8.84 (s, 8 H), 8.25 (d, 8 H), 7.74 (m, 12 H), − 2.72 (s, 2 H).

For the synthesis of Fe-TPP, to a mixture of TPP (400 mg, 0.65 mmol) in N, N-dimethylformide (DMF) (50 mL), FeCl3 (738 mg, 4.55 mmol) was added under N2 atmosphere. After heated to 140 ℃ for 6 h, 200 mL of deionized water was added, followed by the filtration and washing with methanol and deionized water sequentially to obtain the iron porphyrin complex (Fe-TPP).

To a 100 mL flask, Fe-TPP (0.30 mmol), FeCl3 (195 mg, 1.2 mmol), 1,2-dichloroethane (30 mL) and dimethoxymethane (91.3 mg, 1.2 mmol) were added under a N2 atmosphere. After heated at 80 °C overnight, the reaction mixture was filtrated to obtain the black solid, which was then washed with ethyl acetate, dichloromethane, acetone and methanol, respectively. The crude product was purified by Soxhlet extraction with methanol and dichloromethane sequentially for 36 h. The obtained solid was then dried at 60 ℃ for 24 h to afford iron porphyrin-based porous organic polymer (Fe-PPOP).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mimetic oxidase activity assays and kinetic measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The mimetic oxidase activity of Fe-PPOP was evaluated with colorless TMB as substrate. The absorption spectrum of the Fe-PPOP/TMB system was recorded using UV–vis spectrophotometer. Typically, 100 μL TMB (5 mM) and 100 μL Fe-PPOP (1 mg/mL) were added to 2.8 mL of CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (pH = 3.6). The mimetic oxidase activity of Fe-PPOP was measured by monitoring the absorbance (λab=652 nm) of the above system at after holding at 30 °C for 15 min. To optimize the oxidase-like activity of Fe-PPOP, the absorption spectrum of the system was measured at different pH values (2.6–5.4) and temperatures (15–50 °C).

The kinetic measurements were explored by recording the UV–vis absorbance change at 652 nm at different concentration (0.5–4.5 mM) of TMB. Specifically, to 2.7 mL buffer solution (pH = 3.6) with Fe-PPOP (1 mg/mL), 200 μL TMB solution at different concentration (0.5–4.5 mM) was added. The initial rate was then calculated using the Lambert-Beer law (A = ECL, E = 39,000 L/mol/cm). The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) was obtained from the Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal plot
1
ν
=
K
m
ν
max
[ s ]
+
1
ν
max
, where
ν
max
is the maximum velocity, [S] is the substrate concentration, Km is the Michaelis constant, and ν is the initial reaction rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the possible catalytic mechanism of Fe-PPOP
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidase-like activity of Fe-PPOP was probably ascribed to the conversion of dissolved oxygen to reactive oxygen species (ROS) including singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anions (O2 •-), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). To verify this assumption, the catalytic performance of Fe-PPOP was explored under different gas atmospheres including N2, O2 and air. In brief, 100 μL TMB (5 mM) was added to 2.8 mL CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (pH = 3.6). The mixture was maintained at N2, O2 and air atmosphere for a few minutes, respectively. Then, 100 μL of Fe-PPOP solution (1 mg/mL) was injected into the above mixture, followed by the storage at 30 °C for 15 min. To verify the oxidase-like mechanism of Fe-PPOP, the ROS scavenges such as D-histidine, p-benzoquinone, and isopropanol were added to detect the formation of 1O2, O2 •- and •OH, respectively. Specifically, 100 μL scavenger solution with different concentration (5, 10, and 20 mM) was added to the buffer solution (CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (2.8 mL, pH = 3.6)) containing TMB (100 μL, 5 mM) and Fe-PPOP (100 μL, 1 mg/mL). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the resulting mixture was obtained after maintaining at 30 °C for 15 min. Besides, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was conducted to further verify the types of radical species generated in solution.

The colorimetric analysis of S2- was performed according to the following steps. To a 3 mL cuvette, 2.8 mL acetate buffer (pH = 3.6), 100 μL TMB (5 mM), and 100 μL Fe-PPOP (1 mg/mL) were added. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the reaction system was measured after maintaining at 30 ℃ for 15 min 200 μL sodium sulfide solution with different concentration (15–300 μM) was then immediately added into the cuvette. Finally, the corresponding S2- concentration-dependent curve was obtained from the absorbance variation at 652 nm.

To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, 100 μL Fe-PPOP (1 mg/mL) and 100 μL TMB (5 mM) were added to 2.8 mL CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (pH = 3.6). After maintaining at 30 ℃ for 15 min, the absorption spectrum was recorded. Subsequently, various ions including F-, Cl-, Br-, H2PO4 -, NO3 -, OH-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, CH3COO-, SO4 2-, Na+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, and K+ (corresponding to sodium fluoride, sodium chloride, sodium bromide, sodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium nitrate, sodium hydroxide, sodium carbonate, sodium hydrogen carbonate, sodium acetate, sodium sulfate, sodium chloride, ferric chloride, magnesium chloride, zinc chloride, calcium chloride, and potassium chloride, respectively) (200 μL, 3 mM) was added to the above blue solution, respectively. Finally, the absorbance variation at 652 nm was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of sulfide in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The lake water and sewage were selected as the real water samples to measure the concentration of sulfide. Both of lake water and sewage were collected in Wuhan Institute of Technology and used for analysis without further purification. To obtain the relative standard deviations (RSD) and recovery values, 100 μL TMB (5 mM) and 100 μL Fe-PPOP (1 mg/mL) were added to 2.8 mL CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (pH = 3.6). After maintaining at 30 ℃ for 15 min, the absorption spectrum was monitored. 200 μL real water sample was then added to the above solution, followed by the addition of 200 μL Na2S with certain concentration. The absorption spectrum of the resulting solution was measured, and the absorbance variation at 652 nm was recorded to calculate the concentration of sulfide.

Fe-PPOP was prepared through a FeCl3-catalyzed Friedel-Crafts reaction between dimethoxymethane and iron porphyrin, as shown in Scheme 1. Fe-PPOP did not dissolve in the common solvents, such as methanol, chloroform, ethyl acetate, dichloromethane, N, N-dimethylformamide, and water. Comparing with that of porphyrin, Fe-PPOP exhibited excellent thermal-and chemical-stability because of its larger conjugated system and stable framework. As shown in TGA curve (Fig. S1), Fe-PPOP retained nearly 70 % mass even heated at 800 ℃, suggesting its high thermal-stability. The functional groups of Fe-PPOP were verified through Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum. Comparing with that of Fe-TPP, a new signal at 2921 cm−1 was found in Fe-PPOP ( Fig. 1a), which could be assigned to the C-H vibration of the methylene, indicating the polymerization between iron porphyrin and dimethoxymethane happened. Besides, the signal at 1001 cm−1 was observed both in Fe-PPOP and Fe-TPP, which was ascribed to the in-plane bending vibration of N-Fe, indicating the success of iron coordination. The energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) mapping images (Fig. 1b) indicated Fe-PPOP contained the elements of C, N and Fe. The morphology and porosity of Fe-PPOP were verified through TEM and SEM. As shown in Figs. 1c and 1d, Fe-PPOP exhibited a porous structure with a typical lamellar shape.

The porosity and surface area of Fe-PPOP were further explored by nitrogen adsorption and desorption analysis. As shown in Fig. 1e, Fe-PPOP demonstrated a combination of type I and type IV sorption isotherm curve, where the uptake of N2 increased sharply at a relative high pressure (P/P0 = 0.9–1.0), indicating the presence of both mesoporosity and microporosity. The Langmuir and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas were measured as 150 and 92.82 m2/g, respectively. Moreover, the pore size distribution of Fe-PPOP was measured through the nonlocal density functional theory (DFT), which implying Fe-PPOP possessed a wide pore size distribution with the pore widths around 1.8, 2.2, and 2.6 nm, respectively (Fig. 1f). The broad pore size distribution and large BET surface area of Fe-PPOP would facilitate the accessibility of the substrate to the catalytic centers, enhancing its oxidase-like activity.

The chemical bonds and elements of Fe-PPOP was further characterized by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum ( Fig. 2a) suggested the existence of C, N, and Fe elements in Fe-PPOP. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS of Fe-PPOP showed three peaks at 284.6, 285.2, and 285.7 eV (Fig. 2b), which corresponded to C-C, C-N, and CN, respectively. The N 1 s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 397.8, 399.1, and 400.2 eV (Fig. 2c), assigned to pyridinic nitrogen, pyrollic nitrogen, and nitrogen coordinated with iron, respectively. Besides, the Fe 2p XPS spectrum displayed two peaks at 709.4 and 723.2 eV (Fig. 2d), corresponding to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively, further implying that iron was successfully chelated with TPP.

The oxidase mimetic performances of Fe-PPOP were measured with colorless TMB as substrate, which could convert to blue ox-TMB in the presence of dissolved oxygen ( Fig. 3a). In order to evaluate the metal effect on the catalytic activity, the POPs chelated with different metals were prepared. And the absorption spectrum of the different systems (Fe-PPOP + TMB, Co-PPOP + TMB, Zn-PPOP + TMB, and bare TMB) were measured. As shown in Fig. 3b, the color of the Zn-PPOP + TMB and TMB alone systems did not display any significant change after incubation for 15 min. In contrast, the solution of Fe-PPOP + TMB and Co-PPOP + TMB systems gradually turned to blue (insert Fig. 3b), along with the appearance of significant absorption peak at 652 nm. Furthermore, the absorbance at 652 nm of Fe-PPOP + TMB system was stronger than that of Co-PPOP + TMB system, indicating the higher oxidase-like activity of Fe-PPOP. The absorption spectra of the Fe-TPP+ TMB system was also measured as control. As indicated in Fig. 3c, no significant color change of solution was observed in the control system, indicating the Fe-TPP precursor did not display any obvious oxidase-like activity. Therefore, the porous organic framework in Fe-PPOP played an important role in enhancing its intrinsic oxidase-like activity.

The concentration-dependent and time-dependent experiments were performed to further evaluate the optimal activity of Fe-PPOP. As shown in Fig. S2a, the absorbance at 652 nm increased with the concentration of Fe-PPOP. Besides, the absorption intensity enhanced gradually with the incubation time in a period of 80 min (Fig. S2b), indicating the oxidase-like activity of Fe-PPOP is related to the reaction duration and catalyst concentration.

Similar to natural enzymes, pH value and temperature might affect the oxidase-like activity of Fe-PPOP. Thus, the oxidase-like activity of Fe-PPOP at different temperature (15–50 ℃) and pH value (2.6–5.4) were studied. As indicated in Fig. 4a, the relative catalytic activity of Fe-PPOP increased with the pH value changing from 2.6 to 3.6 and then decreased with the further increase to 5.4. The high catalytic activity of Fe-PPOP in weak acid environment was probably ascribed to the diamine structure of TMB, which easily obtained the electron from O2 in the weak acid environment. In addition, the TMB used in the experiment was in the form of hydrochloride, which might decompose and denature in the neutral and alkaline environments. Therefore, the highest relative activity of Fe-PPOP was realized at pH 3.6, which was adopted in the following experiment. Moreover, the relative activity of Fe-PPOP increased from 15° to 30°C, and then decreased from 30° to 50°C (Fig. 4b), which was probably attributed to the lower concentration of dissolved oxygen in higher temperature, resulting in the decrease of the catalytic activity of Fe-PPOP. Thus, 30 °C was chosen as the optimal temperature for Fe-PPOP.

Besides, the catalytic stability of Fe-PPOP was measured through storage stability, pH stability and thermostability experiments. As shown in Fig. S3a, the catalytic activity of Fe-PPOP did not exhibit apparent reduction in a period of 21 days, indicating the high storage stability of oxidase mimetic property. Furthermore, the storage conditions for different pH values did not display any significant influence on the activity of Fe-PPOP (Fig. S3b), while the high temperature could reduce the catalytic activity to some degree (Fig. S3c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis of Fe-PPOP as oxidase mimics
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetic parameter of Fe-PPOP was measured using TMB as substrate under the optimal conditions (pH=3.6 and 30 °C). The experiment was carried out at different concentration of TMB ranging from 0.1 to 4.5 mM, while fixing other parameters constant. Subsequently, the absorbance at 652 nm was recorded in time course mode and the absorbance value was substituted into the Beer-Lambert formula to obtain the concentration of ox-TMB. Therefore, a typical Michaelis-Menten curve was obtained in a certain range of TMB concentration ( Fig. 5a). The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and maximum initial rate (Vmax) were calculated through the Lineweaver-Burk plot (Fig. 5b). The Km and Vmax values of Fe-PPOP using TMB as the substrate were calculated to be 1.098 mM and 4.85 × 10−8 mol/s. The Km value of Fe-PPOP was compared with that of HRP and other reported enzyme mimics. As shown in Table 1, the Km value of Fe-PPOP was relatively lower than that of some reported enzyme mimics, indicating the favorable affinity between Fe-PPOP and TMB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism study of the catalytic activity of Fe-PPOP
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidase mimetic performance of Fe-PPOP was probably ascribed to the generation of reactive oxygen species in the role of oxygen. Therefore, the catalytic activity of Fe-PPOP at different atmosphere was carried out. As shown in Fig. 6a, the absorbance at 652 nm increased to 1.05 after bubbling with O2, while decreased to 0.32 after purging with N2, indicating that the dissolved oxygen played a crucial role in TMB oxidation. To further verify the types of the generated radical intermediates, the ROS scavenging experiments were carried out. As shown in Fig. 6b, the catalytic activity of Fe-PPOP was inhibited with the increasing concentration of p-benzoquinone in the TMB + Fe-PPOP system, while the catalytic oxidation of TMB for D-histidine and isopropanol groups displayed no significant change comparing with that of scavenger-free group. Besides, the catalytic activity of Fe-PPOP was almost completely inhibited with the concentration of P-benzoquinone increased to 20 mM, indicating that O2 •- radical was the key factor to oxidize TMB into ox-TMB. Furthermore, the generated reactive oxygen species were further confirmed by EPR experiment. As shown in Fig. S4, the EPR spectrum using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as the diamagnetic spin trap molecule showed a clear four-line hyperfine pattern of 1:1:1:1 intensity ratio, which was consistent with the signal of DMPO-O2 •- adduct, indicating the generation of O2 •-. These results implied that the Fe-PPOP could catalyze the dissolved oxygen into O2 •- radical, which further oxidized the colorless TMB to blue ox-TMB.

Due to the reductive effect of S2-, it might inhibit the TMB oxidation in the presence of Fe-PPOP, resulting in fading of the blue solution along with the decrease of absorbance at 652 nm ( Figs. 7a and 7b). To confirm this inference, a facile method for S2- detection was developed. The UV–vis spectrum of the Fe-PPOP + TMB system was measured by adding different concentration of S2- (15–300 μM). As shown in Fig. 7b, a decrease of absorbance at 652 nm was observed after the addition of sulfide ions, along with the blue color converting to colorless, probably ascribed to the inhibition of TMB oxidation due to the high reducibility of S2-. Fig. 7c presented two calibration curves of the absorbance change (ΔA, A0-A) at 652 nm with the concentration of S2-. When the concentration of S2- was ranged from 15 to 80 μM, the linear correlation between ΔA and S2- concentration could be expressed as ΔA = 0.00131[S2-] + 0.02828 (R2 = 0.998). And another equation (ΔA = 0.00189[S2-] - 0.02058 (R2 = 0.998)) was obtained ranging from 80 to 300 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) for S2- was calculated to be 1.08 μM by the formula 3σ/k (k: slope; σ: standard deviation), which was comparable to those of reported colorimetric methods ( Table 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the sulfide colorimetric determination method
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of Fe-PPOP toward S2- measurement, the interference experiments were carried out with the addition of F-, Cl-, Br-, H2PO4 -, NO3 -, OH-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, CH3COO-, SO4 2-, Na+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, and K+ to the Fe-PPOP +TMB system. As shown in Fig. 8, the dramatic change of ∆A was observed in the presence of S2-. However, the absorbance at 652 nm of the system exhibited no significant variation with the addition of other interference ions, even increasing their concentration to ten times higher than that of sulfide ions. These results suggested the Fe-PPOP +system could be used as a highly selective platform for the detection of sulfide through a facile colorimetric method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of sulfide in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potential application of Fe-PPOP in environmental monitoring, the concentrations of S2- in lake water, waste water and industrial sewage were measured. The standard addition experiments were utilized to measure the concentration of S2- in the three water samples. As shown in Table 3, the sulfide concentrations measured were consistent with the added values with the recoveries ranging from 92.0 % to 104.7 %, demonstrating the high reliability and accuracy of this method for S2- detection in real water samples.

In summary, a series of metalloporphyrin-containing porous organic framework (M-PPOPs) were synthesized using a facile Friedel-Crafts reaction. Among them, Fe-PPOP exhibited the most excellent oxidase mimetic performance, which could catalyze the oxidation of colorless TMB to blue ox-TMB. Based on the excellent oxidase-like performance of Fe-PPOP, a sensitive, selective and colorimetric assay method was developed for the sulfide detection, with the LOD as low as 1.08 μM. Finally, the Fe-PPOP + TMB system was successfully applied to detect the S2- content in real water samples through a facile colorimetric assay. Thus, this work provided a facile strategy to design and synthesize the highly stable and active metalloporphyrin-based porous organic framework for sulfide detection in real samples with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Linhong Liao: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ding Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Xiaogang Luo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Lingkui Meng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Fengshou Wu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (grant no. 21601142), the Open Project of Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Engineering Process of Ministry of Education (grant no. GCP202116), Outstanding Young and Middle-aged Scientific Innovation Team of Colleges and Universities of Hubei Province: “Biomass chemical technologies and materials” (grant no. T201908), the Innovation Project of Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry (2022BEEA07), and the second batch of the Key Research and Development Project of Hubei Province (grant no. 2020BAB073).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129727.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
